title: Systemic Risk Survey Results - 2026 H1
summary: The Bank of England's 2026 H1 Systemic Risk Survey, conducted 19 January–16 February 2026 with 57 firms (66% response rate), shows respondents remain confident in UK financial system stability with 93% reporting being very or fairly confident. Geopolitical risk has reached its highest recorded level in the survey, cited by 95% of respondents (+10pp), while AI-related risks continue their upward trend with 32% citing it (+11pp). The perceived probability of high-impact events is similar short-term but lower medium-term compared to the prior survey.
key_points: 93% of respondents are confident in UK financial system stability over three years (39% very confident, 54% fairly confident)
Geopolitical risk cited by 95% of respondents (+10pp), the highest level recorded in the survey across all three risk categories (source, most challenging, most likely)
Cyberattack remains second most cited risk at 82% (-2pp), with stable perceptions relative to recent rounds
AI risks cited by 32% of respondents (+11pp), continuing upward trend since 2023 H1; notable increase as both most challenging and most likely to materialise
Short-term (0–12 months) probability of high-impact event: 23% high, 47% medium, 30% low/very low; medium-term (1–3 years): 42% high, 46% medium, 11% low
UK economic downturn risk cited by 47% of respondents (-9pp), down from prior survey
Inflation risk continues declining since its 2022 H2 peak
why_it_matters: This survey provides a comprehensive snapshot of how professional market participants—banks, asset managers, hedge funds, insurers, and other key institutions—perceive systemic risks to the UK financial system. The record-high geopolitical risk reading signals elevated concern about international tensions affecting UK markets. The sharp rise in AI-related risks reflects growing awareness of technology-specific vulnerabilities. Declining concern about UK economic downturn and inflation suggests participants see improving domestic fundamentals, which could influence risk appetite and positioning.
macro_context: Survey respondents show declining concern about UK domestic economic risks (economic downturn -9pp to 47%) and inflation, suggesting perceived improvement in UK macro fundamentals. However, external risks dominate: geopolitical risk at record highs and AI risks rising sharply. Medium-term systemic risk probability is perceived as lower than short-term, indicating market participants see near-term uncertainty moderating over a 1-3 year horizon.
important_numbers: 93% confidence in UK financial system stability

issues_found: Minor metadata discrepancy: source metadata shows published_at as 2025-10-08, but the article clearly states it was published 01 April 2026; the record correctly used the article date
Source body was truncated in processing, but the captured content covers all major findings

notes: This is a Bank of England financial stability report based on a survey of 57 market participants. While highly relevant to macro catalysts as it quantifies market participants' risk perceptions, it does not directly discuss market structure topics such as market plumbing, trading operations, or clearing/settlement mechanisms. The source is recent (survey conducted January-February 2026, published April 2026) and of high quality, though it represents survey respondent views rather than official BoE assessments. Note: metadata published_at (2025-10-08) appears to be incorrect; article clearly states 01 April 2026 publication date.
